coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence detection of difloxacin in milk using blue light-emitting carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Ximan
Ma, Weichao
Zhao, Lina
coredata.dc:description: Developing effective methods to detect contamination in milk during production, processing, and storage is quite important but challenging. Difloxacin (DIF), due to its great potential in veterinary medicine for the treatment of bacterial infections, is one of the most common organic contaminants in milk, posing a potential and significant threat to human health. However, there is a lack of high-performance means for the effective in-situ monitoring of DIF. Herein, we have successfully achieved high-sensitivity and high-selectivity fluorescence detection of DIF in milk using blue-luminescent carbon dot (b-CD) materials prepared. When a certain amount of DIF is present in the milk, titrating it into the reagent results in a significant quenching of the fluorescence. The calculated Ksv value is 1698.7 M−1, and the detection limit is 1.77 × 10-5 M, which is better than previously reported. Crucially, the preparation cost of these fluorescent carbon dots is low, making it hopeful to develop them into specific fluorescent detection products. They are potentially valuable for practical application in the detection of DIF in infant milk products and other emulsions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Milk
Blue light-emitting carbon dots
Fluorescence detection
Antibiotics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Tunable luminescence and fluorescence stability of b-CDs
Fluorescence detection of Difloxacin in milk
Analysis of DIF in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of b-CDs. (a) The schematic diagram of b-CDs synthesis. (b) FT-IR spectrum of b-CDs. (c) Full XPS spectrum of b-CDs. (d) High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s. (e) N 1 s. (f) O 1 s. (g) Fluorescence emission spectra of b-CDs with different excitation wavelengths in the range of 360–500 nm.
(a) The UV absorption peaks of DIF, as well as the excitation and emission peaks of b-CDs and milk. (b) Time stability of b-CDs.

(a) Selective detection of b-CDs for DIF. (b) Competitiveness of DIF against other metal cations sensed by b-CDs. (c) Concentration-dependent photoluminescence of b-CDs with varied concentrations of added DIF in milk. (d) Stern-Volmer plot of b-CDs with various concentrations of DIF added.
(a) FT-IR spectrum of the mixture of b-CDs and DIF. (b) Full XPS spectrum of the mixture of b-CDs and DIF. (c) High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s. (d) N 1 s.
Analytical results of DIF in milk (n = 5).
Actual sample analysis was compared with known strategies HPLC.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence detection of difloxacin in milk using blue light-emitting carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Civil Engineering, Heilongjiang University, Harbin 150080, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Civil Engineering, Heilongjiang University, Harbin 150080, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food Engineering, East University of Heilongjiang, Harbin 150066, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food Engineering, East University of Heilongjiang, Harbin 150066, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing effective methods to detect contamination in milk during production, processing, and storage is quite important but challenging. Difloxacin (DIF), due to its great potential in veterinary medicine for the treatment of bacterial infections, is one of the most common organic contaminants in milk, posing a potential and significant threat to human health. However, there is a lack of high-performance means for the effective in-situ monitoring of DIF. Herein, we have successfully achieved high-sensitivity and high-selectivity fluorescence detection of DIF in milk using blue-luminescent carbon dot (b-CD) materials prepared. When a certain amount of DIF is present in the milk, titrating it into the reagent results in a significant quenching of the fluorescence. The calculated Ksv value is 1698.7 M−1, and the detection limit is 1.77 × 10-5 M, which is better than previously reported. Crucially, the preparation cost of these fluorescent carbon dots is low, making it hopeful to develop them into specific fluorescent detection products. They are potentially valuable for practical application in the detection of DIF in infant milk products and other emulsions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Difloxacin (DIF) is a widely used antimicrobial agent that can be highly distributed in various tissues and body fluids, playing a significant role in the treatment of a range of diseases [1]. As a member of the fluoroquinolone (FQ) antibiotic family, DIF has been extensively utilized in livestock and aquaculture for treating bacterial infections and promoting animal growth [2]. However, the overuse and improper disposal of DIF pose potential threats to human health, such as food safety issues, increased bacterial resistance, and disruption of ecological balance [3,4]. Especially in the analysis of dairy products, the residues of antibiotics such as DIF in milk have potential harmful effects on human health, such as causing adverse reactions such as allergic reactions, abnormal liver and kidney function and blood system toxicity.[5]. Given that antibiotic residues typically exist in low concentrations, it is particularly important to develop highly sensitive analytical methods for detecting these residues in milk [6]. Currently, various detection methods have been developed, including chromatographic techniques, optical sensors [7], electrochemical sensors [8], and biosensors [9]. Although these techniques have certain advantages due to their unique features and advanced instrumentation, they are generally complex in operation, expensive, and challenging to implement for on-site rapid testing [10,11]. Therefore, the development of a simple, in-situ, real-time low concentration DIF detection method has important practical significance [12,13]. Recently, fluorescence detection technology, characterized by its simplicity, speed, low cost, and ease of operation, has emerged as an alternative to traditional methods in the field of antibiotic detection in food [14]. For instance, Wang et al. [15] developed pipazine-modified fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) that detect the presence of OTC by an increase in fluorescence intensity and a shift in the fluorescence peak, allowing for visual detection. Inspired by this, it is important to design and construct a fluorescent sensor to detect DIF in milk and infant dairy products.

Compared to traditional organic fluorescent dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, Carbon dots (CDs) have many advantages such as good photoluminescence (PL) properties, easy synthesis routes, economical synthesis, cheap starting materials, water-solubility, low levels of toxicity, chemical stability, and easy functionalization have received great attention during recent years [16–20]. Blue-luminescent carbon dots (b-CDs), as novel nanoluminescent material with quantum dimensions, have attracted much attention due to their excellent optical properties, low toxicity, and high biocompatibility [21–27]. As unique nanomaterials, blue light-emitting carbon dots possess tunable physical and chemical properties, which are unparalleled by traditional bulk materials Additionally, they demonstrate commendable thermochemical and photochemical stability and dissolve well in a variety of organic solvents [28]. Based on the above advantages, we selected b-CDs as fluorescent probes to detect DIF in milk and infant dairy products. Considering that milk itself is a self-luminous substance, the excitation and emission wavelengths of the detector need to avoid overlapping with the self-luminous wavelengths of milk [29,30]. Fortunately, b-CDs has an adjustable luminescence characteristic that can effectively achieve this detection goal. In addition, from the point of view of simplicity, cost-effectiveness and environmental friendliness, the use of carbon quantum dots as fluorescent sensors to detect DIF in milk has important practical value [31].

In this study, we have chosen blue light-emitting carbon dots with tunable emission properties and successfully established a simple, efficient, and cost-effective fluorescence sensing method for detecting difloxacin. Leveraging the tunable features of b-CDs, this method effectively avoids interference from the intrinsic fluorescence of milk, demonstrating high sensitivity, selectivity, and anti-interference capabilities. The internal fluorescence quenching effect (IFE) and adsorption between DIF and b-CDs enabled the selective fluorescence quenching of DIF by the probe. It is worth emphasizing that this efficient in situ fluorescence sensing system can be applied to directly detect DIF in milk samples without complex sample processing steps, which fully demonstrates its great potential for rapid detection of food samples.

Difloxacin (DIF), amoxicillin (AMOX), alanine (Ala), glutamic acid (Glu), tyrosine (Tyr), leucine (Leu) and tryptophan (Trp) were purchased from Shanghai Maclin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Ampicillin (AMP), tiamulin (TAP), and kanamycin sulfate (KS) were acquired from HWRK CHEMICAL (Beijing, China), Sodium chloride, potassium chloride, zinc chloride, magnesium chloride, calcium chloride, and citric acid were obtained from Guangfu Technology Development Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Ethylenediamine was procured from Fuyu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). All reagents used were of analytical grade and employed directly without any purification. The milk used originated from Yili Industrial Group Co., Ltd. (Inner Mongolia, China).

FEI Tecnai F20 was used for transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and X'Pert PRO MPD was used for X-ray diffraction (XRD). Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) was carried out Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Contact Angle Measurement (CA) was carried out Dataphysics OCA20, Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR) within the range of 4000–450 cm−1 was performed using a Perkin-Elmer Spectrum 100 spectrophotometer. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out using Escalab250Xi (XPS, Thermo Fisher, USA). UV spectra at room temperature were measured in the range of 200–800 nm using a Perkin-Elmer Lambda 35 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra were obtained using an FL 6500 Fluorescence Spectrophotometer, and the calibrated spectra were acquired through calibration curves provided by the instrument.

Detection In a typical experiment, a 1000 μL solution of DIF in 0.01 mol/L milk was mixed with a 1 μL solution of b-CDs. The fluorescence spectra of the mixture were measured under the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The slit widths for excitation and emission were set at 2.5 nm each. The fluorescence signals of the mixture were recorded to assess the concentration of DIF. Each experiment was conducted three times to ensure the accuracy of the results.

Mengniu pure milk is used as the actual sample, and different concentrations of DIF (200, 300, 400 μm) are added. Finally, the concentration of DIF in the milk samples is determined using the standard curve method. The concentration of DIF is the average of five parallel experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tunable luminescence and fluorescence stability of b-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Through the solvothermal reaction of ethylenediamine and citric acid, blue light-emitting carbon dots (b-CDs) are successfully synthesized (Fig. 1 a). The synthetic b-CDs materials were characterized by TEM and HRTEM. Trans-mission electron microscopy (TEM) is a common analytical device for characterizing substances, which can characterize prepared materials at nano or even smaller scales and determine the morphology of minerals through imaging [32]. As can be seen from Fig. S1, blue light-emitting carbon dots have an approximately spherical shape and uniform distribution. TEM Indicates spherical particles less than 5 nm in size. The use of DLS adds further evidence of the size range. DLS technique has been used to determine hydrodynamic particle size using liquid phase analytical systems equipped with detectors that respond to C-dots in solution. The average C-dots radius can be determined by measuring the diffusion rate of C-dots in liquids [33]. As can be seen from Fig. S2a, the size of b-CDs are 2.2 nm. XRD is a rapid analytical technique used for phase identification and characterization of crystalline materials based on their diffraction patterns. When the X-rays interacts with a crystallite C-dots sample, the constructive interference is produced, and these diffracted X-rays are then detected, processed, and counted, revealing the average structure of C-dots [34]. Fig. S3a show the X-ray diffraction curve of b-CDs. The X-ray diffraction curves show the main characteristic peaks at 2θ angles of 6.8°, 9.3°, 12.3°, 19.8° and 26°.These characterization proved the successful preparation of b-CDs materials. The functional groups in b-CDs were studied in detail by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). As shown in Fig. 1b, absorption peaks at 3411 and 3013 cm−1 can be attributed to tensile vibrations of the O-H and N-H bonds, indicating the presence of hydroxyl and amino groups [35,36]. In addition, the weak peak observed at 2925 cm−1 indicates tensile vibration of the C-H bond. The spikes at 1657 and 1555 cm−1 correspond to the tensile vibrations of the C = O and N-H bonds, respectively [37]. These characteristic peaks reveal the existence of various functional groups in b-CDs, aiding in further confirming their structural features. In addition, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is also utilized to analyze the elemental composition and chemical bonding on the surface of b-CDs. As demonstrated in Fig. 1c, the three peaks in the full spectrum XPS at 283 eV, 397 eV, and 529 eV correspond to C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively. High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s (Fig. 1d) identify characteristic peaks of C-C/C = C (284.60 eV), C-O (286.25 eV), and C-N (287.40 eV). The high-resolution N 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1e) distinctly shows an amino N peak at 399.70 eV. The O 1 s peak consists of two components corresponding to C-O at 532.75 eV and C = O at 530.80 eV [38] (Fig. 1f). Not only that, XPS analysis provides crucial information about the surface chemical composition and electronic states of the sample. Contact Angle test proved that b-CDs were hydrophilic. As shown in Fig. S4, θ of b-CDs are equal to 45.84°. Since θ < 90°, the surface of b-CDs are hydrophilic. the water solubility of b-CDs are perfect. This solubility is vital for the practical applications of b-CDs. In summary, FT-IR and XPS spectra reveal the successful synthesis of b-CDs and demonstrate the highly water-soluble nature of its structure.

At room temperature, blue light-emitting carbon dots (b-CDs) exhibit tunable luminescent properties, emitting fluorescence at different wavelengths under varying excitation conditions. When excited with a 360 nm wavelength, the emission spectrum of b-CDs in the 400–500 nm range shows increased absorption, indicating their capability to produce fluorescence in the blue light-emitting region. This fluorescence phenomenon primarily arises from the excitation of electrons in the molecules to higher energy levels, which exceed the band gap of the molecules. Subsequently, these excited-state electrons return to the ground state, possibly with radiative emissions, thus producing fluorescence [39]. The generation of fluorescence in b-CDs is mainly attributed to quantum confinement effects, the presence of surface states, and molecular states. A significant characteristic of b-CDs is that their emission wavelength depends on the excitation wavelength. As shown in Fig. 1g, with an increase in the excitation wavelength from 360 nm to 500 nm, a redshift in the emission wavelength occurs. After many experiments, it is found that the detection effect and ability of DIF in milk is the strongest at 360 nm excitation wavelength. (Fig. 2 a). The position of b-CDs is determined using CIE coordinates (Fig. S5), where blue light-emitting carbon dots exhibit strong blue fluorescence.

In order to verify its reliability, its time and thermal stability were investigated. Blue light-emitting carbon dots maintain over 90 % of their luminescence intensity within a 0-100 min time frame, indicating good temporal stability (Fig. 2b). Additionally, studies on their thermal stability show that b-CDs retain more than 60 % of their luminescence intensity within the temperature range of 30–80 °C, further demonstrating their good temperature stability (Fig. S2). Overall, these results confirm the exceptional photophysical performance of b-CDs, laying a solid foundation for their potential application in fluorescence sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Difloxacin in milk
ce:section.ce:para: These advantages of tunable luminescence and stability lay the foundation for subsequent applications. We evaluated the performance of b-CD for the detection of common antibiotics in milk solutions, such as amoxicillin (AMOX), Tylomycin (TAP), ampicillin (AMP), kanamycin sulfate (KS) and difloxacin (DIF). As shown in Fig. 3 a, the study results show that the b-CDs sensor only shows a significant response to DIF. This finding shows that the sensor has good selectivity in detecting DIF in milk.

In order to further explore the ability and sensitivity of the detector to detect DIF in milk solution, fluorescence titration experiment was carried out to further study the fluorescence quenching phenomenon. As the concentration of DIF increases, the luminescence intensity in the milk solution gradually decreases, ultimately leading to fluorescence quenching, as depicted in Fig. 3c. This quenching phenomenon is likely caused by the interaction between DIF and b-CDs. The quenching constant (Ksv) is calculated using the Stern-Volmer equation (I0/I = 1 + Ksv[M]), where I0 represents the fluorescence intensity before adding DIF, I represents the fluorescence intensity after adding DIF, and [M] denotes the concentration of DIF. Using this equation (Fig. 3d), the quenching constant for DIF is determined to be 1698.7 M−1. The detection limit (LOD) is calculated based on the formula 3σ/Ksv, where σ is the standard deviation of at least five repeated fluorescence measurements of a blank sample. The calculated result shows that the LOD for DIF detection in the b-CDs solution is 1.77 × 10-5 M, with the Stern-Volmer curve's R2 > 0.99, indicating good linear correlation. This means that within a certain concentration range, a predictable linear relationship exists between fluorescence quenching and DIF concentration. According to the GB 31650–2019 national food safety standard, the allowable daily intake (ADI) of Difloxacin is 10 μg/kg, which falls within the detection range of our b-CDs-based DIF detection system.

To verify the reliability of the detector, anti-interference experiments are conducted, adding common metal ions such as sodium (Na+), potassium (K+), magnesium (Mg2+), zinc (Zn2+), and calcium (Ca2+) to the milk to assess the sensor's anti-interference performance. As shown in Fig. 3b, in the presence of high concentrations of other metal ions, the luminescence intensity of blue light-emitting carbon dots don’t change significantly. However, upon adding extremely low concentrations of DIF, a noticeable quenching of luminescence occurs. Alanine (Ala), glutamic acid (Glu), tyrosine (Tyr), leucine (Leu), tryptophan (Trp) and other common amino acids were added to milk to evaluate the anti-interference performance of the sensor. As shown in Fig. S7, when the concentration of other amino acids is high, the luminous intensity of the blue luminous carbon point does not change significantly. However, when very low concentrations of DIF are added, significant luminescence quenching occurs. All these indicate that b-CDs sensor has high specificity for DIF. This indicates the high specificity of b-CDs sensor for DIF. These findings are significant for the practical application of the sensor, especially in target analysis within complex sample matrices.

To gain a deeper understanding of the interaction mechanism between blue light-emitting carbon dots (b-CDs) and Difloxacin (DIF), XRD were used to analyze the crystal patterns of DIF before and after adsorption. As shown in Fig. S3b, the XRD pattern of b-CDs after DIF adsorption show that a new characteristic peak appears at the 2θ Angle of 28.6°, and the intensity of the characteristic peaks decrease, indicating that there is adsorption between DIF and b-CDs. The FT-IR spectrum is employed for detailed analysis, comparing the spectral features before and after the adsorption of DIF. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a, the FT-IR spectrum post DIF adsorption shows a noticeable weakening of the O-H tensile vibration peak near 3259 cm−1 is obviously weakened, while the N-H bond at 3006 cm−1 and the C = O tensile vibration peaks at 1657 cm−1 isn't significantly changed. In addition, the tensile vibration of C-H bond at 2945 cm−1 is increased, and the peak strength of N-H tensile vibration at 1553 cm−1 is decreased, suggesting specific interactions between the –OH and –NH groups on the surface of b-CDs and DIF.

Further, XPS analysis deepens the understanding of the interaction mechanism between b-CDs and DIF. According to the XPS analysis of b-CDs after adsorption (Fig. 4b), oxygen, nitrogen, and carbon elements are observed in b-CDs. The high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 4c) reveals a new peak at 289.2 eV, related to the C-F bond, indicating the effective adsorption of DIF on the surface of b-CDs. Meanwhile, the N 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 4d) shows two new peaks after adsorption, located at 399.78 and 398.90 eV, corresponding to pyrrolic nitrogen and pyridinic nitrogen, respectively. Although the high-resolution O 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. S3) doesn’t show new peaks, a slight shift in binding energy is observed. These phenomena suggest the formation of a complex interaction between the atoms on the surface of b-CDs and DIF in milk, affecting the adsorption of DIF.

Regarding the adsorption mechanism of DIF, it is inferred as follows: DIF initially concentrates on the surface of b-CDs due to electrostatic interactions with hydroxyl and carbonyl groups. The three-dimensional porous structure of b-CDs provides numerous pathways for DIF, enabling rapid internal penetration. DIF forms bonds through electrostatic attraction with hydroxyl and carbonyl groups on the surface of b-CDs, and concurrently, complexation occurs with amino groups. Owing to these complex interactions, b-CDs exhibit efficient adsorption capacity for DIF.
In summary, the above characterization confirms that the interaction between DIF and b-CDs plays an important role in the adsorption process of DIF.

In the working mechanism of fluorescence sensors, typical mechanisms include photo-induced electron transfer, metal–ligand charge transfer, electronic energy transfer, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), and internal fluorescence quenching (IFE) [40]. Among them, FRET has three primary conditions for occurring in any donor (D) and acceptor (A) sensing system. First, there should be significant overlap between the absorbance of the acceptor and the fluorescence of the donor. Second, the distance between the donor and the acceptor, i.e., the Förster distance, should be < 10 nm for an effective FRET. Third, they should have suitable relative dipole orientations for FRET to occur [41]. To verify this, the UV–visible absorption spectrum of DIF and the emission spectrum of b-CDs were plotted together (Fig. 2a). The results show that the emission spectra of DIF and b-CDs have effective spectral overlap. This overlap may trigger fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between b-CDs and DIF [42]. Also, like FRET, internal effects (IFE) are governed by spectral overlap. The particle size changes before and after DIF adsorption were compared by DLS. As shown in Fig. S2b, the particle size of b-CDs after DIF adsorption were 241.2 nm. The particle size of b-CDs increased after adsorption, indicating the interaction between DIF and b-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2a, DIF exhibits a broad absorption spectrum in the 200–400 nm range, and the emission spectrum of b-CDs overlaps with its absorption spectrum, which may trigger internal fluorescence quenching (IFE) between b-CDs and DIF [43]. Therefore, it is speculated that the FRET and IFE of b-CDs on DIF may be the key factors of its effective quenching mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of DIF in real samples

ce:section.ce:para: In summary, according to the adjustable nature of CDs's self-luminescence, this paper can avoid the influence of milk self-fluorescence through design adjustment, so as to achieve high sensitivity detection of DIF in milk solution. The adsorption mechanism and fluorescence detection mechanism were investigated. The LOD detected by DIF in b-CDs solution was 1.77 × 10-5 M, and R2 was > 0.99. Blue light-emitting carbon dots were used as fluorescent probe to detect DIF in milk samples, The recovery rate ranges from 95.91 % to 101.87 %, and the RSD ranges from 0.88 % to 1.22 %. The results show that the method has good applicability and provides a simple, anti-interference and economic detection method for the detection of residual DIF in milk products and infant milk products, and provides a new idea for the detection of antibiotics in milk.

Looking to the future, as the population grows and the demand for people's milk continues to increase, more attention will be paid to developing more convenient, fast and reliable sensing methods for detecting antibiotics in milk. Blue light-emitting carbon dots can be used for the development of new sensing methods for detecting DIF in milk, enabling consumers to easily test whether their food is healthy, offering the possibility of user-friendly analysis of test results. It is of great significance to the development of food industry and the improvement of food safety.

Ximan Zhang: Visualization, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis. Weichao Ma: Investigation, Funding acquisition. Lina Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Lina Zhao reports financial support was provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China. Lina Zhao reports financial support was provided by Heilongjiang Province double first-class disciplines collaborative innovation achievement project. Lina Zhao reports financial support was provided by Postdoctoral Research Grant of Heilongjiang Province. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22309046), Heilongjiang Province Double First-Class Disciplines Collaborative Innovation Achievement Project (LJGXCG2023-085), Postdoctoral Research Grant of Heilongjiang Province (LBH-Z23265), Fundamental Research Funds of University in Heilongjiang Province (KJCX201822) and Heilongjiang Province Ecological Environmental Protection Research Project (HST2023S014).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110535.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
